evals.prompt: Queremos criar a mensagem de oferta para o nosso serviço de implementação de IA para PMEs — 30 dias, resultado garantido. Precisamos de uma landing page e de uma proposta comercial. Por onde começamos?
evals.expected_output: A KB-first lookup for existing offers and ICP data, a five-anchor offer context, the Grand Slam Offer four-lever analysis with specific claims and evidence, a value stack totalling ≥ 3× the investment, a guarantee type selection, and the 17-step Sabri Suby scaffold populated.
expectations: The skill calls kb_search for existing offers, landing pages, ICP data, and any prior win/loss notes before authoring anything — the Rebranding-2026-06 verbal identity is surfaced if available.

The five offer anchors are established before any copy is written: product/service (what exactly is being sold, deliverable, timeline, outcome), ICP (specific vertical/role/company size/trigger event), single biggest pain, dream outcome (in client's words), and competing alternatives — missing anchors are requested before proceeding.

The Grand Slam Offer four-lever analysis is produced: Dream Outcome (specific, measurable, emotionally resonant — not 'AI automation' but 'your team completes onboarding in 3 days instead of 3 weeks'), Perceived Likelihood (specific credibility evidence — case studies, mechanism, or risk reversal), Time Delay (first win within the first week named), Effort and Sacrifice (specific burdens removed, 'done-with-you' framing).

The value stack table is produced with all components and perceived values, total ≥ 3× the actual investment — the fast-action incentive is real (not faked), and the investment figure is flagged for fin:pricing-redesign alignment before publishing.
The 17-step Sabri Suby scaffold is fully populated (no placeholder steps) and the NUEPH scorecard is produced: N/U/E/P/H each green or red with gap notes for any red.

evals.prompt: A nossa taxa de conversão em propostas está abaixo de 15% — os prospects dizem que o preço é alto mas não pedem desconto, só desaparecem. O que está errado na proposta?
evals.expected_output: A diagnosis that the missing element is a risk reversal (guarantee), not a price problem; a guarantee design appropriate to the offer; and the risk reversal positioned prominently in the proposal structure — with CFO + CEO gate flagged before committing.
expectations: The skill names the SKILL.md red flag: 'Missing the risk reversal — without a guarantee, the prospect absorbs all the downside; conversão will stay below 15%.' The below-15% conversion rate is matched directly to this red flag.

The diagnosis distinguishes the real problem: the 'price is high' objection from prospects who then disappear without asking for a discount is a risk-perception problem, not a price objection — the prospect is not convinced the outcome justifies the risk, not that it is unaffordable.

A guarantee is designed from the four types (Results guarantee, Effort guarantee, Pilot/trial, Satisfaction guarantee) — the strongest one the company can credibly stand behind is recommended with an explicit rationale.
The guarantee is written in plain language (one sentence, no buried conditions) and its placement in the 17-step scaffold is specified: Step 11 (Risk reversal), prominent, impossible to miss.
The CFO + CEO gate is flagged before committing to a results guarantee — the company must be able to absorb the financial downside; this is not a CMO-only decision.

evals.prompt: Criámos uma value stack para o nosso serviço mas o total só chega a 2× o preço de venda. O que fazemos para a tornar mais convincente?
evals.expected_output: A diagnosis that the value stack fails the 3× rule, specific guidance on which component types to add or reframe (real standalone value only — no phantom bonuses), and a revised stack totalling ≥ 3×.
expectations: The skill names the SKILL.md red flag: 'A value stack where the total is less than 3× the investment — the offer must feel like undeniable value; if it does not, add components or reduce the price (CFO gate).'

The diagnosis identifies which value stack rule is being violated: either the components are not truly standalone-valuable (phantom bonuses inflated to make the math work) or real components exist but are not being unbundled and presented as standalone items.

Specific component types are recommended to add real standalone value: a training/workshop component, a template library, a quarterly review session, an async support package, access to a community or peer group — each must be something the ICP would pay for separately.

The revised stack is produced with all components listed, perceived standalone values assigned (not inflated), and total ≥ 3× the actual investment — with a note that the investment figure requires fin:pricing-redesign alignment before publishing.
The NUEPH scorecard is run on the offer: the 'U' (Único) and 'E' (Exclusivo) scores are checked — a low NUEPH score on Único means the value stack is commoditised even if it passes the 3× rule.
